questions.q: Identify what the security-usability paradox states.
options: Adding more or harder security steps always makes a system more resistant to attackers
Security and usability are completely separate concerns that never affect one another
If security is too difficult or inconvenient, users circumvent it and their workarounds create new vulnerabilities, making the system less secure overall
Usable systems are inherently insecure because convenience requires removing controls
questions.explanation: The paradox is that more friction is NOT more secure: when security is hard to use, users work around it, and those workarounds introduce new vulnerabilities, so usability is itself a security requirement. The chain to remember is 'hard to use, so worked around, so insecure'. Option A is the named trap — the naive belief that more steps equals more security — which the paradox directly refutes.

questions.q: According to the episode, along which four axes do end users vary, requiring secure design to be inclusive?
options: Speed, Security, Storage, and Scalability
Skill, Device, Context, and Ability
Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, and Disclosure
Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, and Authentication
questions.explanation: Users vary along Skill (technical understanding), Device (smartphone-only logins exclude some users), Context (public Wi-Fi, shared family computers), and Ability (accessibility for users with disabilities). Option C lists parts of STRIDE — threat modelling from the previous episode — and option D lists security properties formalised next chapter, so both are real syllabus terms applied to the wrong question.

questions.q: Using the strict 16-character monthly password policy example, explain why the policy backfired.
options: The passwords were too short, so attackers guessed them by brute force
Users could not remember the passwords, so they wrote them on sticky notes or made predictable variations like 'Password1' then 'Password2', making credentials easier to steal and guess
The encryption protecting the stored passwords was outdated and was cracked
The policy applied only to administrators, leaving ordinary user accounts unprotected
questions.explanation: The complexity policy exceeded users' capability to comply, so they bypassed it — writing passwords in plain sight and using predictable increments. The human workaround created a worse vulnerability than weak passwords would have, decreasing security rather than increasing it. This is the security-usability paradox in action: difficult security became bypassed security.

questions.q: What does the guiding principle 'secure default, easy right path' mean?
options: The system should default to the fastest option and let advanced users enable security manually
Security settings should be hidden from users so attackers cannot find them
The safe option is enabled by default so an inactive user is still protected, AND the secure way to complete a task is also the easiest way, so the path of least resistance is the secure one
Users must complete a security tutorial before the default settings unlock
questions.explanation: 'Secure default' means a user who does nothing is still protected without needing expertise; 'easy right path' means the secure way is the easiest way, so users have no incentive to find an insecure shortcut. Naming both halves is the mark. Option A inverts the principle by making security an opt-in for experts, which the episode explicitly rejects.

questions.q: The four supporting design features are hooked as the verbs Show, Scale, Survive, Guide. Which feature does 'Survive' represent?
options: Transparency — showing users their protection status, such as a lock icon
Progressive security — matching security strength to the risk of the action
Contextual guidance — showing password rules as the user types
Recoverable security — assuming people make mistakes and giving safe recovery paths instead of a permanent lockout
questions.explanation: 'Survive' is recoverable security: it assumes users make mistakes and provides account recovery options rather than a permanent lockout that could lose someone a financial-aid deadline. Option A is 'Show' (transparency), option B is 'Scale' (progressive security), and option C is 'Guide' (contextual guidance) — all four are real features in the mnemonic, but only Survive matches the recovery-from-mistakes idea.

questions.q: Distinguish between a user persona and an abuse case in secure design.
options: A user persona models malicious misuse, while an abuse case models legitimate goals
Both model legitimate users, but a persona is more detailed than an abuse case
A user persona profiles a legitimate user's goals and context to keep security usable, while an abuse case describes how a malicious user might misuse a feature to keep security effective
A user persona is a marketing tool, while an abuse case is a legal compliance document
questions.explanation: A persona asks 'what does Alex want to do?' to keep security usable; an abuse case asks 'how would an attacker exploit this?' to keep security effective — good design needs both, modelled simultaneously. Option A reverses the two definitions, which is the most tempting trap because both terms sound similar. User-centred design is not naive: you design for the legitimate user and against the abuser at the same time.

questions.q: Why is the answer 'tell users not to write passwords down and train them to follow the policy' considered the weak response to the sticky-note scenario?
options: It is too expensive to deliver training to every employee
It blames the user for a design failure and changes nothing, because the policy remains unusable
It does not mention encryption, which is the real cause of the breach
Training is effective, but it should be delivered annually rather than once
questions.explanation: The weak answer is the named trap: it blames the human for a design failure instead of fixing the design, so the unusable policy stays in place and the bypass continues. The strong answer reframes the failure as a design problem — allowing passphrases, removing arbitrary changes, adding live guidance and MFA — so the human succeeds. 'Design failure, not user failure' is the whole lesson.

questions.q: A banking app forces full multi-factor authentication for every single action, including checking an account balance. Applying the episode's framework, what is the most appropriate improvement?
options: Remove all authentication so the app is faster and more convenient to use
Keep the heavy authentication everywhere, because more friction always means more security
Apply progressive security (Scale): use a fast PIN or biometric for low-risk balance checks and escalate to full MFA only for high-risk actions like large transfers
Force users onto a single smartphone-only login method to standardise the experience
questions.explanation: Progressive security matches authentication strength to the risk of each action, so frequent low-risk tasks stay frictionless while irreversible high-impact actions get stronger checks. Option B repeats the 'more friction equals more secure' trap, which causes users to circumvent the control. Option D fails the Device axis by excluding users without a suitable smartphone.

questions.q: A failing student tries to change their own grade through URL manipulation. Which design responses best address this abuse case?
options: Authorisation checks and audit logging, so the action is blocked and any attempt is recorded
A complex password policy and frequent forced password changes
Data minimisation and a privacy policy update
A responsive layout and faster page-load times
questions.explanation: The grade-tampering abuse case is countered by authorisation checks (verifying the user is permitted to perform the action) plus audit logging (recording the attempt) — the accountability and authorisation properties met formally next chapter as part of CIA-AAA. Data minimisation (option C) is the response to the identity-thief harvesting abuse case, not grade tampering, so it targets the wrong threat.

questions.q: Explain why the episode argues that 'usability is itself a security requirement' rather than merely a convenience.
options: Because usable software loads faster, and speed reduces the window for attacks
Because security controls that frustrate users get circumvented, and the workarounds introduce new vulnerabilities, so a system users cannot use correctly is less secure in practice
Because accessibility laws require all software to be usable by people with disabilities
Because usability testing is the cheapest phase of the software development life cycle
questions.explanation: Usability is a security property because the most technically secure design can be the least secure system in practice if real users bypass it — and their bypasses create vulnerabilities worse than the original threat. This is the band-six insight at the heart of the episode. Option C is true in general (it links to inclusive design) but it is not the reason usability counts as a security requirement here.
